name: Paul Quinichette
abstract: Paul Quinichette (May 17, 1916 – May 25, 1983) was an American jazz tenor saxophonist. He was known as the "Vice President" or "Vice Prez" for his emulation of the breathy style of Lester Young, whose nickname was "The President", or simply "Prez".

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Charlie Parker Memorial Concert
New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Music of Count Basie

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Schaap
about_quotes.evidence: I played Milestones at the West End on March 11, 1974 with Paul Quinichette and Matthew Gee, and Sam Woodyard.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Schaap interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 9, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions 'Paul' multiple times, but context clearly identifies him as Paul Chambers ('Then you get to Paul [Chambers], which is another genius... We had a good time. We worked with Miles... The trio was so together... We made some records with Wes Montgomery.' and 'Philly Joe [Jones]
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Cobb — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Percy Gabriel
about_quotes.evidence: That session happened to be held in the Down Beat. We was playing that again. ... With Charlie? Well, he was in on that session too. It was Duke Ellington, Dorothy Donegan, Art Tatum, and Jay McShann, Coleman Hawkins. Charlie Parker was playing alto. And Paul Quinichet on tenor.

about_quotes.speaker: Count Basie
about_quotes.evidence: INTERVIEWER: Let me ask you this: would you possibly -- you couldn't possibly have Paul Quinichette and Wardell Gray Delagray together? [INTERVIEWEE] Oh, yeah. I don't know, I don't see why I couldn't, would you?

about_quotes.speaker: Freddie Green
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Marshall Royal, Ernie Wilkins, Eddie Lockjaw, Paul Quinichette, Charlie Fowlkes, Jimmy Lewis, and Gus Johnson. [INTERVIEWEE] Mm hmm. Was Foster in that band?

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Mabern
about_quotes.evidence: Jimmy Jones, who did orchestrations for Duke, who played on that famous record with Sarah Vaughan with Clifford Brown or Joe Benjamin or Paul Quinichette and Herbie Mann.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Mabern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 31, 2010
